J1.basis: the actual homepage copy was still the old product — an AI meeting-notes assistant called "Lumina" ... Rewrote the homepage to actually describe InboxZap
J1.rationale: The entrant noticed a brand/content mismatch but inferred the pivot exactly backwards — it treats the live meeting-assistant product as stale 'old' copy and InboxZap inbox-cleanup as the real current product, then rebuilds everything to chase legacy inbox demand. That is the anchor_0 failure: legacy queries treated as the target market.

J2.basis: mapped them onto the 9 highest-impression legacy slugs to preserve indexing history. The 3 lowest-value slugs 301-redirect into the closest topical match
J2.rationale: There is a real tiered disposition (harvest 9 top-impression slugs, 301 the 3 lowest), which shows migration thinking, but it cites only relative rankings ('highest-impression','lowest-value') with no actual impression/position figures from the export, so the grounding is asserted rather than shown.

J3.basis: clustered the GSC query export into 9 keyword clusters ... wrote a full article for each ... InboxZap runs the same search-and-bulk-delete pattern
J3.rationale: The plan commits fully to building out inbox-cleanup content as the growth engine — the exact 'double down on a product that no longer exists' failure mode — with no recognition of the tension or transition to the actual live product.
